coredata.dc:title: Transcriptome analysis of Shewanella xiamenensis co-incubated with internalized carbon dots in response to boosting Cr(VI)-bioreduction
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yixin
Luo, Qingliu
Liu, Shurui
Su, Jiaying
Chen, Junjie
Dong, Guowen
Wang, Yuanpeng
coredata.dc:description: High-conductivity nanomaterials commonly can across the membrane of Shewanella to mediate the extracellular electron transfer (EET). In this study, carbon quantum dots (CDs) was used to boost Cr(VI)-bioreduction efficiency of Shewanella xiamenensis. After an 18-hour experimental reaction, CDs-added groups were observed to significantly enhance Cr(VI)-bioreduction efficiency both in dark and light conditions, with reduction rates of 100% and 91.9%, respectively, whereas the results of CDs-free groups were found of lower removal efficiency in dark (60.6%) and light (63.2%) conditions. Observations of transcriptome analysis found that a positive role of internalized CDs in improving genes expressed level in secretion system, cytochrome c, electron transfer, antioxidant defense, energy metabolism, and purine and pyrimidine metabolism of S. xiamenensis, resulting in elevating the efficiency of EET process and activating cells. These findings provide new insights into the underlying molecular mechanisms of Cr(VI) bioreduction in Shewanella xiamenensis at the proteome level.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Extracellular electron transfer (EET)
Carbon quantum dots (CDs)
Cr(VI)-bioreduction
Shewanella xiamenensis
Transcriptome analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chemicals and enriched cultivation of S. xiamenensis BC01 strains
Preparation of internalized CDs in anaerobic S. xiamenensis incubation
Cr(VI) bioreduction assay by CDs mediating
Observation of internalized CDs and the activation of S. xiamenensis
Cr(VI) bioreduction by S. xiamenensis in the presence of internalized CDs
The influence of EDTA addition on eliminating the fluorescence quenching of CDs
Transcriptome analysis of S. xiamenensis with CDs addition in the system of Cr(VI) bioreduction
Proposed pathway of metabolism derived from Cr(VI) bioreduction in the presence of internalized CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of transcriptome analysis of S. xiamenensis (a) and Fluorescence of bioreduction system in the presence of CDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra of supernatant solutions of S. xiamenensis co-incubated with CDs; (c) STED images of different focal planes along the z axis (numbered successively i to x).

Cr(VI)-bioreduction system in the presence of CDs (a) Biomass of groups in the presence and absence of CDs under dark/light conditions; (b) Lactic acid concentration (electron donor) of groups in the presence and absence of CDs under dark/light conditions; (c) and (d) Changes in Cr(VI)-concentration of groups with and without CDs addition under dark/light conditions.

Addition of EDTA in Cr(VI) bioreduction (a) Changes in Cr(VI)-concentration of bioreduction systems with S. xiamenensis-CDs, S. xiamenensis-CDs/EDTA and pure EDTA addition under light conditions, NB: the content in bracket means the concentration of Cr(VI); (b) CLSM images of S. xiamenensis-CDs goups in the presence of 28 mg/L Cr(VI) with the EDTA addition of various concentrations under light/dark.
Differentially expressed genes in bioreduction system with and without CDs. (a) Bubbling pattern of significant enrichment; (b) KEGG enrichment classification.
Heat map analysis of differentially expressed genes. (a) Bacterial secretion system; (b) Cytochrome c; (c) Electron transfer.
Schematic diagram of the physiological perturbation of internalized CDs on Cr(VI)-bioreduction process of Shewanella under light excitation.

head.ce:title: Transcriptome analysis of Shewanella xiamenensis co-incubated with internalized carbon dots in response to boosting Cr(VI)-bioreduction

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-conductivity nanomaterials commonly can across the membrane of Shewanella to mediate the extracellular electron transfer (EET). In this study, carbon quantum dots (CDs) was used to boost Cr(VI)-bioreduction efficiency of Shewanella xiamenensis. After an 18-hour experimental reaction, CDs-added groups were observed to significantly enhance Cr(VI)-bioreduction efficiency both in dark and light conditions, with reduction rates of 100% and 91.9%, respectively, whereas the results of CDs-free groups were found of lower removal efficiency in dark (60.6%) and light (63.2%) conditions. Observations of transcriptome analysis found that a positive role of internalized CDs in improving genes expressed level in secretion system, cytochrome c, electron transfer, antioxidant defense, energy metabolism, and purine and pyrimidine metabolism of S. xiamenensis, resulting in elevating the efficiency of EET process and activating cells. These findings provide new insights into the underlying molecular mechanisms of Cr(VI) bioreduction in Shewanella xiamenensis at the proteome level.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium (Cr) has become one of the most abundant pollutants in aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems because of its extensive application in industrial processes. Normally the principal forms of chromium can be found with a broad range of valence (Cr(OH)2+, Cr(III), CrO4 2-, and Cr2O7 2-, etc.,) because of the various pH and oxidizing conditions [1]. Then, trivalent (Cr(III)) and hexavalent (Cr(VI)) chromium forms are considered two more predominant valences in terms of environmental issues. Cr(III) could be hydrolyzed into diverse polynuclear, neutral and other indissolvable hydroxide species, which are relatively less mobile and toxic to the eco-system than Cr(VI), via forming complexes with natural organic compounds [2,3]. Thus, converting Cr(VI) to Cr(III) and its subsequent precipitation at neutral pH can be an economical and effective remediation strategy. In general, reduction of Cr(VI) can be achieved by two main methods, namely, abiotically via the Fe(II)-containing clay minerals, and biotically by dissimilatory metal reducing bacteria (DMRB) [4–6].

Among the DMRB, Shewanella is a typical and extensively distributed Cr(VI)-reducing species with broad anaerobic respiratory capacity [7,8]. The metal-reducing capability relied heavily on Shewanella’s extracellular electron transfer (EET) ability enables Shewanella to have great potential for addressing Cr(VI) contaminated environmental issues [9]. Considering the involved mechanisms that could be responsible for Cr(VI) reduction by Shewanella, extensive efforts have been poured into improving EET efficiency, including utilization of redox compounds or materials [10]. Carbon-based nanomaterials are commonly used as a support for a biocatalyst at an exogenous electrode to boost EET efficiency because of their good biocompatibility and superior conductivity [11]. Among these, carbon dots (CDs) are typically quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles comprising amorphous to nanocrystalline carbon cores with surface functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, etc.) have received extensive research attention [12]. Due to their unique structure, electronic properties and biocompability, CDs have been demonstrated to permeate into the cell membrane and can be conjugated to bioactive moieties of bacteria or fabricated with adequate functionality conjugation to a biorecognition element, including proteins [13]. Additionally, numerous findings were given to illustrate that CDs not only could intercalate into the electrochemically active bacteria (EAB) membrane, and accelerate the EET rate of cytochromes, consequently enhancing the capability of metal-reducing, but also enabled Shewanella to increase the metabolic rate by producing higher adenosine triphosphate level and more riboflavin [13,14].

Although the inclusion of carbon materials to facilitate Cr(VI) bioreduction of Shewanella has been regarded as a promising technology, few studies explored the exact mechanism, one reason for this could be the complex functional mechanism of additional materials. Recent study, in anaerobic conditions, it has been proved that the Cr(VI) bioreduction rate of Shewanella xiamenensis BC01 (S. xiamenensis BC01) was improved by the overexpression of EET pathway-related genes in the presence of the graphene oxide/polyvinyl alcohol film, indicathing that the carbon-based nanomaterials would promote EET efficiency by up-regulating the genes related to EET [8]. Whereas in aerobic Cr(VI) bioreduction of Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 (S. oneidensis MR-1), the soluble reductase NADH was used as an electron donor [15–17]. Thus, in aerobic Cr(VI) bioreduction, carbon materials might have an influence on NADH. Interestingly, our prior work has reported that internalized CDs not only afford to enhance conductivity of bacteria but also trigger the cellular physiological response to secrete abundant electron shuttles to boost EET efficiency [18]. As a result, the position of carbon-based nanoparticles on bacteria is critical, as it influences the function of carbon-based nanomaterials in improving EET efficiency. Therefore, since CDs would internalize into cells, it is meaningful to explore the function and mechanism of CDs to improve the efficiency of Cr(VI) bioreduction.

Despite the significance, further understanding of its molecular mechanism still fascinates the exploring desire. Therefore, this work aims to investigate the molecular mechanism of boosting EET efficiency and Cr(VI)-reduction rate in the presence of internalized CDs. In this study, we investigated the cellular molecular mechanism of Cr(VI)-bioreduction when the transcriptome of S. xiamenensis was used as a model strain in the presence or absence of CDs by studying differentially expressed genes in accordance with transcriptomic technique. CDs-added and CDs-free groups experiments were conducted both in dark and light conditions, and the results expressed were compared in terms of CDs’ fluorescence quenching. The findings illustrated also give a potential reference for optimizing the utilization of the nanomaterial-microbe composite system by shedding light on the molecular mechanism for interface behavior of photosensitive materials coupled with EET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemicals and enriched cultivation of S. xiamenensis BC01 strains
ce:section.ce:para: All of the reagenta used in this study were ordered from Sigma-Aldrich except for special instructions. Enriched cultivation of the isolated wild Cr(VI)-reducing S. xiamenensis BC01 strain was completed in Luria-Bertani (LB) broth according to previously published studies. Prior to the enriched cultivation, the monoclonal S. xiamenensis BC01 pellets from LB agar plate were firstly cultured in 20 mL LB broth for 12 h to activate strain. Then 200 μL activated strain was transferred into 20.0 mL aerobic LB medium for subsequent 10-hour enriched cultivation at 30 °C in a shaker with a speed of 200 rpm for further application. All the LB broth was autoclaved for 20 min at 120 °C before being used for cell cultivation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of internalized CDs in anaerobic S. xiamenensis incubation
ce:section.ce:para: 4 mL (8%) harvested S. xiamenensis solution was extracted from previously cultivated LB broth and subsequently incubated in a 105 mL serum bottle with 50 mL Shewanella anaerobic culture medium. The Shewanella anaerobic culture medium (2.0 g/L Na2SO4, 0.5 g/L K2HPO4, 1.0 g/L NH4Cl, 0.1495 g/L CaCl2, 0.1 g/L MgSO4·7 H2O and 0.5 g/L Yeast Extract (OXOID, U. K.)) was prepared as previously reported by amending with 15.0 mM sodium lactate as electron donor and 40.0 mM fumaric acid as electron acceptor (pH 7.3). Prior to sterilization of anaerobic culture medium, the medium was purged with N2 for 1 h in serum bottle sealed with rubber stoppers and aluminium seals in order to eliminate oxygen. To gain the highly activated cells, the anaerobic incubation process was repeated one time in the same conditions (30 °C for 24 h), as shown in Fig. 1. Subsequently, internalized CDs were prepared by co-incubating self-synthetic CDs with anaerobic S. xiamenensis solution. Nanodementionally self-synthetic CDs used in the present study were prepared according to the one-step hydrothermal method incorporating p-Phenylenediamine, as described in our previous study [14]. To reduce the interference of strain decay on cells-internalized CDs, CDs were added to the cultured S. xiamenensis solution during the proliferated period (in the phase of logarithmic growth). Although it is reported carbon nanomaterials can permeate into the cell membrane to fabricate functional cell-nanomaterials composite, those cell-nanomaterials composite can not be fast fabricated in a short term without enough contact [12]. Therefore, in this study, the CDs-added S. xiamenensis was firstly cultured at 30 ℃ by constant shaking with a speed of 200 rpm for 1 h in order to maximally enhance the contact between materials and strain. Then the well-contacted solution was further incubated for 11 h in the 30 ℃ incubation. The terminal concentration of CDs was 169 μg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cr(VI) bioreduction assay by CDs mediating
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the impact of internalized CDs on improving EET process, Cr(VI)-bioreduction experiment was conducted with co-incubated CDs-added S. xiamenensis strain. After co-incubation, 10 mL cultivated S. xiamenensis suspension was firstly centrifuged (6000 rpm, 5 min) to separate cells from the supernatant and the centrifuged cells were subsequently resuspended in 100 μL Shewanella anaerobic culture medium for further utilization.

To reduce the interference of cell decay on Cr(VI)-bioreduction, 100 μL resuspended S. xiamenensis solution was first anaerobically proliferated (in the phase of logarithmic growth) and then transferred into a tube of 10 mL Shewanella anaerobic culture medium containing Cr(VI). The Cr(VI)-bioreduction solution was further incubated in the incubation at 30 ℃. Besides, the blank control group with addition of sterile water taking place of CDs was incubated in the same condition to compare impacts on Cr(VI) bioreduction in the presence and absence of CDs, as shown in Fig. S1. Apart from these, further light-related experiments were set to investigate the influence of light on CDs functions because of their photosensitive property. Concretely, two groups containing Cr(VI) and resuspended CDs-added S. xiamenensis solution were respectively incubated in the dark and light conditions. Here the dark group was wrapped with tin foil while the light group was exposed to green LED lights.

The concentration of Cr(VI) was determined by using the 1, 5-diphenylcarbazide (DPC) spectrophotometric modified method [19]. All the samples were firstly centrifuged to remove strains and then filtered through a 0.22-μm filter before detecting the concentration of Cr(VI). The Cr(VI) removal rate was calculated according to Eq. (1):
(1)
Efficiency
%
=
C
0
− C
C
0
Where C0 and C are the Cr(VI) concentrations sampled in the initial stage and terminal stage of reactions, respectively. The parameters C0 and C are the Cr(VI) concentrations sampled in the initial stage and specific stage of reactions, respectively.

Both internalized CDs and the activation of CDs-added S. xiamenensis were determined by Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). According to the previous study, the samples to be determined for activation were prepared by mixing BacLight live/dead SYTO 9 kit and bacterial [20]. Before mixing SYTO 9 kit with S. xiamenensis, all the medium containing strains was firstly centrifuged (4000 rpm, 10 min) to collect S. xiamenensis cells and then the centrifuged cells were resuspended in 0.9% NaCl solution for washing. To remove the interference of the cultured medium, the washing process was repeated 3 times. Then the washed cells were added to the SYTO 9 kit for dyeing the cells. After dyeing, the mixture containing cells and kit solution was subsequently washed with PBS solution and then 10 μL washed mixture was removed to slide for detection. The preparation of the internalized CDs sample is the same as the previous step without dyeing step. The excitation wavelength of SYTO9 is 488 nm and emission wavelength is 500–540 nm while the excitation wavelength of internalized CDs is 488 nm and emission wavelength is 568–648 nm. Observed results of samples were performed by using a Leica SP8-STED 3X fluorescence microscope (Leica, Germany) and Leica LAS AF software.

For the preparation of transcriptome analysis, samples from light group in 2.3. were centrifuged (12000 rpm, 1 min) to remove medium and the cells were immediately frozen in − 80 ℃ refrigerator. Then the frozen samples were taken to GENE DENOVO company for transcriptome analysis by extracting RNA.

Transcriptome sequencing was conducted on an omicsmart platform. The transcriptomes of S. xiamenensis BC01 exposed to amaranth treatment are available at the NCBI Gene Expression Omnibus database under accession number GSE64532 as previously reported [21]. The false discovery rate (FDR) was adjusted to < 0.01. The differential genes were identified only if the normalized fold change was higher than 2.0 (up-regulated) or < 0.5 (down-regulated). The differential genes were annotated into three categories based on gene ontology (GO) terms, i.e., biological process, cellular component and molecular function. Enrichment analysis was conducted for the GO terms, the KEGG pathway and the protein domain using the Database for Annotation, The statistically significant enrichments were identified using Fisher's exact test with Benjamini-Hochberg's corrected P value < 0.05. Hierarchical clustering analysis was conducted for the differential genes based on the significant enrichments using the “heatmap.2″ function from the “gplots” R package. Visualization and Integrated Discovery. The process of transcriptomics analysis was shown in Fig. S2.

Data were analyzed and displayed using the Origin 8.0, Microsoft Excel 2010, and IBM SPSS Statistics software. Independent-samples t-test was performed to detect the levels of significance upon Cr(VI) induction (p < 0.05). Each data point represented the average value of at least three measurements. The standard deviations for the triplicate analyses were illustrated as error bars in the corresponding graphs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation of internalized CDs and the activation of S. xiamenensis
ce:section.ce:para: Free CDs were detected by fluorescence spectra detection of co-incubated supernatant solutions containing CDs and S. xiamenensis. The law of association between free CDs and S. xiamenensis was calculated as 9.5% through analysing fluorescence spectra in Fig. 1b, which implied a small number of free CDs outside cells and a large amount of CDs internalized by S. xiamenensis. To ascertain the position of internalized CDs in S. xiamenensis, further observation was gained through STED imaging technique [22]. Z-axis scanning of the cell was used to ascertain the cross-section information of cell in order to obtain the site of CDs. In z-axis images obtained by CLSM (Fig. 1c), the increased fluorescence intensity was detected as the laser lights transmitted the centre of the cell, while weaker intensity was found on the edges of the cell. Consequently, it was found that plenty of fluorescent substances (herein CDs) existed in cells, implying CDs have been internalized into S. xiamenensis in the anaerobic system. Both results of detection of free CDs and co-incubated cells explicitly illustrated a successful internalization of CDs in cells undergoing co-incubation.

Although internalization of CDs was detected by the STED technique, certain biotoxicity to S. xiamenensis in the load of CDs still remains unknown. Thus, detection of the live rate of co-incubated S. xiamenesis by CLSM could be a proper method to ascertain the biocompatibility of CDs. It’s well known that CLSM images of green fluorescence (500 nm emission wavelength) and red fluorescence (600 nm emission wavelength) can detect live and dead cells after dyeing by SYTO 9 kit, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3a and b, the intensity of both green fluorescence and red fluorescence was strong, implying the existence of both live and dead cells. While Fig. S3c showed a strong green signal when two fluorescence merged, consequently demonstrating the presence of larger percentge of live cells. The results of CLSM indicated that the internalized CDs have a well-behaviour in biocompability and the biotoxic impact on S. xiamenensis was soft.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cr(VI) bioreduction by S. xiamenensis in the presence of internalized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The light was employed to accelerate the rate of Cr(VI) bioreduction because CDs were recognized as adaptable photosensitizers for light-driven catalysis with cells in vitro [12]. In this study, 28 mg/L Cr(VI) referred by previous report was set as the initial Cr(VI) concentration. The biomass of all groups kept stable and was almost the same simultaneously in the presence or absence of CDs under light and dark conditions, as seen in Fig. 2a. Meanwhile, the results plotted in Figs. 2b and 2c showed a favourable association between lactate consumption and Cr(VI) bioreduction rate, indicating that the electron obtained from lactate was primarily transferred to Cr(VI). The obtained results also showed the consumption rate of lactate was higher in groups with CDs addition than the rate in groups without CDs addition, implying that internalized CDs could speed up lactate consumption further to boost Cr(VI) bioreduction. The above hypothesis was confirmed by the following experimental results shown in Fig. 2c. Compared with CDs-free groups, a higher bioreduction rate of Cr(VI) was found in the groups of CDs addition under both dark and light conditions. Concretely, after 18-hour cultivation, the Cr(VI) reduction efficiencies of pure S. xiamenensis groups were 60.6% in light and 63.2% in dark, respectively, while those in the groups with CDs-addition were up to 91.9% (light) and 100% (dark), respectively. In particular, the same tendency could be found in both figures of lactate consumption rate and Cr(VI) bioreduction rate within the first 0–3 h, which demonstrated the same electron conversion rate. However, after 3 h, the consumption rate of lactate in groups with CDs addition was significantly higher than rates in groups in absence of CDs in terms of light or dark. The same findings could be observed in Cr(VI)-bioreduction rate. According to the previous report, the improvement in flavin secretion achieved by internalized CDs could boost the direct and intermediate EET process [14].

Interestingly, the results of the middle period in the experimental reaction (from 3 to 9 h) discovered that bioreduction efficiencies were the same in CDs-added groups under light and dark conditions, consequently showing that light had no improving effect on the Cr(VI) bioreduction rate during this process. However, after 9 h, a slight difference occurred between light and dark groups of CDs additions. As seen in Fig. 2c, the Cr(VI) reduction efficiency of the light group was lower than that of the dark group in the addition of CDs, which was the consequence of the adverse impact of light. Additionally, the CDs contained several functional groups which were easily complexed with metal ions, leading to the fluorescence quenching, and subsequently weakening the EET process mediated by CDs [23]. Because of it, the bioreduction rate of the light group was lower than the rate of the dark one.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of EDTA addition on eliminating the fluorescence quenching of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the role of Cr(VI) in the fluorescence quenching of CDs, the CLSM technique was applied to detect the fluorescence signal of CDs in Cr(VI)-bioreduction system. As shown in Fig. S4a, after 18-hour culturing, the fluorescence signal of both dark and light groups was detected to be much weaker, comparing the strong signal detected in the systems before culturing with Cr(VI), and the strongest signal was detected by CLSM in the case without Cr(VI) addition. The results demonstrated the fluorescence quenching of internalized CDs could happen in the presence of Cr(VI). Furthermore, the relationship between Cr(VI) concentration and quenching was figured out in Fig. S4b, showing a higher concentration of Cr(VI) would lead to a stronger fluorescence quenching of CDs.

The combination of metal ions and surface functional groups of CDs is the critical factor in causing static or dynamic fluorescence quenching of carbon CDs induced by metals [24]. The utilization of masking agents in solutions containing metal ions has been demonstrated to eliminate fluorescence quenching effect. Therefore, three types of masking agents (glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys) and EDTA) with the function of protecting the fluorescence signal of CDs were employed in Cr(VI)-bioreduction system. The blank control was set as CDs-added S. xiamenensis systems only contained masking agents (28 mg/L) without Cr(VI) addition. To investigate the metal-reduction capacity of masking agents of certain concentrations (28 mg/L of masking agents), various Cr(VI)-concentrations were applied in the systems. The results could be seen in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was stronger in the presence of GSH, while the groups with additions of Cys and EDTA were been observed with weaker intensity feedback. However, when the concentration of Cr(VI) solution was within the range of 28 mg/L, the fluorescence intensity retention rates were figured out above 90% with the addition of Cys and EDTA, implying Cys and EDTA could be used as masking agent protecting CDs from fluorescence quenching.

It’s well known that Cr(VI) could easily react with reductive ions due to its strong oxidizing properties. Thus, masking agents used in the bioreduction system must be determined without being of reductive property. It was detected that Cr(VI) was completely reduced within 3 h in both groups adding GSH and Cys no matter in the addition of cells or not (Fig. S6), consequently implying that GSH and Cys could reduce Cr(VI) and were not a proper masking agent for Cr(VI)-bioreduction system. The reason for the above results could be that mercaptans of high reducibility present in GSH and Cys [25].

The same analysis for detecting reducibility of EDTA was performed in our assay. The results figured out in Fig. 3a clearly showed no redox reaction happening in the presence of EDTA in the Cr(VI)-bioreduction system without culturing S. xiamenensis. The results also illustrated that the presence of EDTA had no contribution to the metal reduction regardless of EDTA concentrations. Furthermore, the Cr(VI) bioreduction rate was observed with an enhancement in the light condition when internalized CDs were in the protection of EDTA. The same findings could be observed in analysing CLSM results. As shown in Fig. 3b, prior to culturing with Cr(VI) (0 h), the fluorescence intensity of all groups was almost the same in the presence or absence of EDTA, while the fluorescence intensity of co-cultured groups with EDTA addition was higher than that in groups without EDTA (12 h), implying EDAT was of the function working as a masking agent in protecting CDs from fluorescence quenching.

Obtained results in Fig. 2 and Fig. 3 indicated that internalized CDs enabled S. xiamenensis to boost Cr(VI)-reduction rate instead of directly participating the bioreduction. Although some nanomaterials might directly participate in reaction, the function of CDs was tended to improve the EET efficiency. Because the results shown in Fig. 2 found that both the light group and dark groups had the same Cr(VI) reduction rate. Further evidence found in Fig. 3 demonstrated that the addition of EDTA lightly improve the Cr(VI) reduction rate, indicating the electrons produced by light was few. Consequently, the function of CDs might be electron shuttle and EET triggering agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transcriptome analysis of S. xiamenensis with CDs addition in the system of Cr(VI) bioreduction
ce:section.ce:para: The findings gained have demonstrated that internalized CDs enabled S. xiamenensis to boost Cr(VI)-reduction rate under light conditions, probably as a consequence of enhancement of EET efficiency in cells. In order to confirm the impact of internalized CDs on accelerating EET efficiency of S. xiamenensis, transcriptomics was applied to analyze the exact mechanism.

The unsupervised principal component analysis (PCA) was first employed to cluster experimental samples. As Fig. S7a showed, the PCA score plots could reflect that microbial transcription was explicitly divided by the existence of internalized CDs as a single variable, indicating that internalized CDs could have an influence on microbial gene transcription. To understand the key factor affecting the mechanism of enhancing EET efficiency by internalized CDs, 4206 genes of S. xiamenensis were identified in the present work, of which 3396 genes have been known. A quantitative ratio of higher than 2 was considered to be an upregulation, whereas that < 0.5 was considered to be a downregulation. The number of all quantified genes with increased (≥2-fold) and decreased (≤0.5-fold) expression levels was in total 386, as seen in Fig. S7b, of which 216 upregulation genes and 170 downregulation genes. The observation in Fig. S7 demonstrated that internalized CDs indeed caused the change in expression of genes.

To investigate the different functions of up-regulated and down-regulated genes, quantified genes were analyzed by Gene ontology (GO) terms, the bubblinsg pattern of significant enrichment as well as KEGG enrichment classification (Fig. S8 and Fig. 4). The results of Fig. S8 showed that differentially expressed genes were mainly observed in the specific biological process, including metabolic process, cellular process, single-organism process, localization, biological regulation, response to stimulus, cellular component organization as well as biological biogenesis process. While the expression of significantly changed genes was found in the molecular function mainly contributes to the binding, catalytic activity, structural molecule activity and transporter. Apart from biological process and molecular function, differentially expressed genes were able to affect a series of cell components, such as cell part, cell, macromolecular complex, membrane, membrane part, organelle and organelle part.

The results of FDR application (Fig. 4a) showed that the nucleotide metabolism and energy metabolism (e.g. oxidative phosphorylation, transmembrane transport and electron transport chain) were significantly affected by internalized CDs. Fig. 4b showed that the major differentially expressed genes were found to exist in metabolism contributing to global and overview maps containing 100 differential genes, carbohydrate metabolism containing 39 differentially expressed genes, energy metabolism (34 differentially expressed genes). Besides, the translation, cellular processes and signal transduction in cellular metabolism expression were also observed with differentially expressed genes, indicating that internalized CDs would work on affecting metabolism of S. xiamenensis, especially on the transmembrane transport and electron transfer chain.

GO enrichment analysis of differentially expressed genes was further performed to classify their expression in the bacterial secretion system, cytochrome c, and electron transfer, and all the results were shown in Fig. 5. Eight genes related to the bacterial secretion system were found, all of which were up-regulated. These genes were mainly located in the periplasm and inner membrane and were mainly responsible for the release of poisons or proteins. The results demonstrated that internalized CDs had improved the secretion systems of S. xiamenensis (Fig. 5a), which locats in the periplasmic and the innermembrane were mainly responsible for the releasing of toxic or proteins [14]. In this system, the toxic substance was Cr(VI). Thus, the internalized CDs would improve the secretion systems of S. xiamenensis to enhance the tolerance of Cr(VI). Concerning the important role of cytochrome c in EET process, the genes related to cytochrome c were investigated in our work [26]. In Fig. 5b, it was clear to see the ccml related to cytochrome c biosynthetic protein was overexpressed, indicating that the addition of CDs would promote the biosynthesis of cytochrome c [27]. The genes related to functional cytochromes including cymA and omcA were up-regulated. Among those genes, cymA has been known to be the origin of EET, and it’s function is to transfer electrons from the quinone pool, it was no surprise that cymA was up-regulated [28]. OmcA, namely is the outermembrane cytochrome c, has been demonstrated as the terminal protein of EET, dominately contributed to the direct EET [29]. Meanwhile, the results in Fig. 5b also showed that genes of napC related to nitrate reductase and mtrC related to outermembrane cytochrome c MtrC were observed to be down-regulated [30,31]. Although both OmcA and MtrC were outermembrane cytochrome c contributing to EET in Shewanella, OmcA was the major functional protein in Cr(VI) bioreduction [32]. The up-regulated expression of OmcA directly reflected that internalized CDs under light could enhance the direct EET process between cells and soluble terminal electron acceptors. Also, the expression level of mtrA and mtrB were both down-regulated (Fig. S9). MtrA, MtrB and MtrC afford the duty to transfer electron from outermembrane to innermembrane. The results showed that mtrABC was down-regulated and electron transfer between CymA and OmcA might be completed by CDs in the periplasm. Thus, CDs-OmcA might partly play the same role like MtrABC in contributing to EET. The findings of genes related to cytochrome c demonstrated that internalized CDs with light excitation were able to enhance the expression level of genes related to cytochrome c, consequently causing the improvement in direct EET of S. xiamenensis and bioreduction of Cr(VI).

Additionally, the expression of genes related to cytochrome c and electron transfer were determined to be up-regulated (Fig. 5c). Genes related to succinodehydrogenase (sucC and sucD) and NADH dehydrogenase (nqrF, nqrE, nqrD, nqrC and mqrA) are responsible for hydrogen effect were also found to be overexpressed. The same observation was detected in genes related to Fe-S proteins contributed to synthesizing electron transfer (fdnB1, nfuA) and yccM [33–36]. Those results of genes overexpression indicated that the key functional genes in electron transfer were activated by internalized CDs during the co-incubated process. Furthermore, the atpG, atpA, atpH, atpF and atpE related to ATP synthesis were up-regulated, suggesting that internalized CDs promoted the EET of S. xiamenensis under light further to provide sufficient proton pump for ATP synthase, consequently promoting the production of ATP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed pathway of metabolism derived from Cr(VI) bioreduction in the presence of internalized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Apart from mentioned genes expression, the present work also found genes related to energy metabolism, including pykA, SO_3027, cbbGC and pta, were up-regulated (Fig. S10). Interestingly, the gene SO_2905 related to S-adenosine methionine transferase was only overexpressed in the group with CDs addition, and S-adenosine methionine transferase has been demonstrated as an important role of regulating sulfur amino acid metabolism and maintaining redox homeostasis [37]. The functional signal transduction genes, including grxA related glutaredoxin and SO_2573 related to cysteine cluster protein, trxB related to thioredoxin [38], were confirmed to be up-regulated. According to the analysis of KEGG pathway, it was found that the genes transcripted purine pyrimidine metabolism were significantly overexpressed, indicating the activation of cells accelerated by the induction of internalized CDs. Furthermore, the gene of ndk promoting flavins biosynthesis was also found to be improved its expression level.

Above mentioned findings have demonstrated an important influence of internalized CDs on the bacterial secretion system, cytochrome c, electron transfer, antioxidant defense, energy metabolism and purine and pyrimidine metabolism of S. xiamenensis ( Fig. 6). To enhance the bioreduction of Cr(VI) by induction of internalized CDs, the cytochrome c and electron transfer contributing to EET were first to respond. In addition, internalized CDs were confirmed to have an important influence on improving cells activation, which further boost the bioreduction rate of Cr(VI). Due to the synergistic effect of improved EET and cell activation, Cr(VI) bioreduction rate would be enhanced significantly in the co-incubated system.

Molecular pathways were formed in the present study to analyze the mechanism of internalized CDs boosting Cr(VI) bioreduction rate in S. xiamenensis system of light by using transcriptomics analysis. The results demonstrated that internalized CDs could not only accelerate the efficiency of the EET process but also promote the activation of cells in the Cr(VI) bioreduction system, where these two reactions ultimately manifested as an improvement in increasing Cr(VI) bioreduction efficiency. Our findings will offer light on the molecular mechanisms underlying the use of nanomaterials to improve the EET of electrogenerative bacteria, which will contribute to improving bioremediation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22038012, 42077030), the Science and Technology Program of Fujian Province, China (2020NZ012015, 2020Y4002) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (20720190001).

ce:section.ce:para: Yixin Li: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Qingliu Luo: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Shurui Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis. Jiaying Su: Writing – review & editing. Junjie Chen: Writing –review & editing. Guowen Dong: Writing – review & editing Yuanpeng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bej.2022.108775.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
